speaker: interviewer
text: Part three with Wanda Mehr.

speaker: interviewee
text: Germany's very interesting.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's just quickly review-- But I'm not. I'm sorry. I'm far from Australia. That's right. Let us just review very quickly your time in prison. How long were you in prison for all together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Half a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: From when to when?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, if I have to say a month, I never-- I think there was no time there. The whole world was still in the-- there was no time. No one thought of a month. It was all forgotten.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was after the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: I know that I've been half a year. That I know. But a month, to think of what month it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was it after the ghetto, the prison?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course. I was already with the Polish woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, so was it winter or summer when you started?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I think it was in between. It must have been in-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] no, it wasn't summer. I think it must have been in end of winter. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think so, because when they took me to prison, I wore a coat. So it wasn't summer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's say--

speaker: interviewee
text: Is it so important?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, we just get a rough idea, probably February '43?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it must have been end of '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: End of '42.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, because in '43, I was already in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Till about May '43.

speaker: interviewee
text: About, yes, could be, because it all happened in '42 through the whole year. Two weeks on in ghetto, and now with the Polish woman a few weeks. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That was actually-- she took the child home already, I remember. And before I left for Germany-- that was before, half a year before, because I went out. And I had some-- I don't know where I kept, I had some that was small change Polish money. And I wanted to buy a little robe for my daughter. And I went out. And then I never came back for half a year. And they-- from the streets they got me to prison. I said first to the Ukrainian [INAUDIBLE]. And from there, they took us all to the [INAUDIBLE] street, where the prison was a half a year. And when I came back, then she told me-- when I was released from prison, the priest helped me. I told this story before. Then I came back to her. And I told her what happened. She didn't know that I was in prison. She thought I disappeared from the street like many people disappeared on the street. If some German thought of only one thought that maybe she is Jewish, they took away people. Even Poles, they killed if they suspected only anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I came back, then she told-- I told her the story, that those police is after me, that I came-- when I left the prisons they went after me. And I went to the church. And later went out, and the priest helped me in the church too. One night when I came back, he said, now, when you're out of prison, and my daughter started to cry. She wanted her mommy.

speaker: interviewee
text: But she said, no, no, you can't. You just have to go. The police is after you. Then as I said before, when they-- if they come here and see a child, then they will be sure that you are Jewish. And they would kill me too. Better you go out on the street [INAUDIBLE] they are surrounding people of the street and taking them to German to forced labor. And you go to Germany. You write me from there. And that was it. So that's how it happened with the prison, half a year I was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were in prison, you were talking last time that--

speaker: interviewee
text: How they tortured me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, is it two or three times you were before the Gestapo, is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, at night time, and how the conditions were there that in a small room we were about more than 100 people, women only.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you only interrogated two or three times in six months?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the beginning, when they hit me, and they took me into the office where Gestapo, chief Gestapo was there, and the chief, and how they hit-- but these were SS men, not Gestapo. He was Gestapo, sitting behind his desk, but he said nothing. He couldn't tell them, because also he was a top Gestapo man. But SS had more to say than he himself. There were young man from the SS. And they were awful. They tortured actual-- they tortured people. And the first time they took me in, and ask me questions. But I didn't answer, because [INAUDIBLE] I don't understand German. They knew that Jews-- most Jews understand German. If it's a good German or bad German, but they could speak German. And Poles couldn't. So I didn't answer. They hit me a few times in face. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I bleeded. But I didn't cry. I forgot to cry, how to cry. And I think that helped me, because they knew that if a Jewish girl-- I was a girl there, they didn't know that I have a child.

speaker: interviewee
text: When they tortured them, they cried, and started to beg them. I didn't. I just thought millions of thoughts in a second, what will happen? Will they kill me? Will I ever see my little child? What will she do if they kill me alone there? Will the Polish woman keep her? And-- but I didn't faint.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was stronger than ever, not eating for such a long time, not even sleeping. Standing, we slept standing, one woman against the other with the backs, because there was no room to sit down. And I have seen others in that second split of a second, I have seen before my eyes the picture of other women getting out from there nearly cut to pieces with shreds of skin hanging, bleeding. And-- but I didn't faint. I thought to myself, you can't faint. Then they will kill you. And finally, they let me go, and pushed me back into the room. I think those women inside were already conditioned. They never reacted on anything. They were-- hour by hour, they took people in, and questioned and hit them, and tortured them there. And then they put them back.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kinds of torture? Did you know what kinds of torture the other women had?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't know. It was razors or anything like. I've seen only one woman. And she was a Russian, could be Jewish too, because when the Russian army left in a hurry, they left behind their wives. They couldn't take them. And the wives were left. But then the Germans came. And they took over after the Russians were pushed out. And a lot of them, they imprisoned. And it could be there were Jewish among them. And I only have seen-- but I have been told later that that was not a prison, because there was another prison in [NON-ENGLISH] on the [NON-ENGLISH]. The street was known because it was for hard criminals that was at prison. But the prison that I was was more for political prisoners. And that's how the wives of those Russian mostly officers, because other soldiers were not allowed to bring their wives. The officers, yes, so that's how they landed in prison. They took them as wives of the Russians who fled Russian officers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, what sort of torture? I don't know what they use there. But I have seen a woman coming-- and there were many-- just cut on their bodies, half naked. They took off-- tore off-- their clothes. And the skin was cut.

speaker: interviewee
text: I've seen hanging, just rips of skin hanging, and overlapped, and how they pushed it in. But what they did inside, I don't know. I know what they did to me, that they came in middle of-- I still have on my head, I still have scars. And I had to show my scars here to a doctor. Dr. Meyer was his name, on [INAUDIBLE] Road, I remember, when I applied for German restitution.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you survive most of your time in the prison?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you survive most of your time in the prison?

speaker: interviewee
text: In prison?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know. I'm not very religious. My family was not, as I said, traditional more, but not very religious. But I not believing in miracles, I can't think of anything else. My survival from the beginning, the child rescued, how I went for a whole night and walked with her to the hospital, and that doctor brought me to the border office, because it was not where I was behind the-- when I left the ghetto, I wasn't allowed out to go out-- to go out of the front gate. So I went to the back. And it was more than three kilometers. And how I got out there, I don't know. Sometimes I think of miracles. But I know there are no miracles. No miracles happened to save us all there. And, yet, something-- it was unnatural that I survived all this. I can't believe that I could do the things that I did there. I couldn't. I wouldn't have the courage.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you wearing-- What were you wearing in the prison? What sort of clothes did you have with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had the clothes that I left my home on myself, never washed. No one thought of washing. We didn't have even good drinking water in prison when I was. So I can't think-- I don't even-- I remember everything. But I don't remember what I-- I wore, because-- to cover myself. It must have been torn. I remember that the Polish woman gave me, when I was with her, gave me to look like a peasant woman. And she took away whatever I had. I had still a good coat on me, because it was cold winter, though she took it from me and gave me a peasant skirt, something, an old-- it didn't have any color. It was so old that there was no-- you couldn't say what color it was. But a white peasant skirt to go to be-- to go out on the street where they should have taken me to Germany. So I had a skirt, something, and an old blouse, that was all, and a scarf on my head, that what she gave me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the prison? Have you got any strong memories of what the prison looked like?

speaker: interviewee
text: In prison?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, can you describe the inside, what it looked like?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, it was timeless. No one thought of time there. I never knew what hour of the day it was. The time passed so quickly. When they took me-- when they came in middle of the night, and hit me on my head with something very hard, that when I-- later on when [INAUDIBLE] questioned me, I was standing sleep-- sleeping standing. I don't know if I slept or dreamt. But one came in, one could be SS men, and he hit me something. He was very tall. He towered above me as I was standing. I thought I got shorter there, because not eating, not doing anything. He just hit me with something hard on my top of the head, and ask me-- they did it purposely at night time during sleep so people don't, perhaps, remember. But I did remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember that was-- they ask me what's your name in German? And I have to answer. If I wouldn't answer, he would hit me again. And I was bleeding, the blood running over my face. It was still-- scars are still there. I can touch them. And so it was running over my face. And he didn't care about anything. He asked me again, what's your name? And not even thinking of anything, I said Wanda Grabowienska.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I think that was something like a miracle that I didn't say Ida Spiegler. I didn't. Half asleep, I said Wanda Grabowienska. And then he left me. And as he left me, I couldn't do anything. I just put my hand in. And I thought, it goes in right into my brain. But no, it was only a cut on top. But it wasn't deep the bone. The bone was not broken.

speaker: interviewer
text: Previously, you said that you had to describe what the prison looked like.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you still do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was a very tall building. I think there were three stories, or maybe more. I don't know if more. A tall gray building, a big building, a huge building, and near the street, near the pavement. And we went in in a heavy entrance door. A group of people that we came with a truck, I think I said how they put us on a truck. And one man, he must have been Jewish, tried to flee. He just jumped off. And they shot him on the-- as he jumped off, they shot him straight away, because the truck was-- the people was guarded by SS.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I thought, how silly? He could wait. I would never do that. It's always time to kill myself, or to be killed to the last minute, as I always did.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we came right to the-- it was a-- street was [NON-ENGLISH]. That's why the name of the prison was-- I know everybody called it [NON-ENGLISH]. And we went in. And we went up, I think it was second floor. And with me, a few people. Others, I don't know, maybe to different-- how it was inside, I never checked it. I have no idea. I know only it was second floor. And just with one push, they pushed me in, opened the door and pushed me in. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Last time, you were talking about when they left you out of prison, you were on a-- you found yourself on a park bench with the policeman. What happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: First, I went-- the first thing I felt-- I'm very sensitive, and especially during the war. I felt behind, that someone's behind me. And I turned my head. And I've seen two policeman in civil tiny clothing to a toll man. They did as they walked just right the street, but I knew that they after me. So instead to go to the-- to see my child, to the Polish woman, it was not far from there. But that was not the church from which I had this birth certificate. The church I got the birth certificate was entirely different place. That was the first-- just a church that I passed. So I went in. And I thought, let them think that you are going to thank Jesus or Maria for-- that you were released from prison.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went in. But I knew that they are not moving from there. They waited for me. They just put into their heads that I am Jewish. And I was released. So I stayed in the church longer, much longer. The priest, whoever was there, left. The priest noticed me, came up.

speaker: interviewee
text: First, he had a look around. No one was there. I think he wanted to close the church. It was late. And told me, he said, yes, child, I know. Don't say anything. He understood. He took me somewhere and hid me somewhere there for the night only, and gave me even a piece of bread, and hid me somewhere there. I think it was like his cellar. He said, you stay here. In the morning, they will leave for the night. They won't stay here a whole night. He understood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the church?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was [NON-ENGLISH]. That is something [NON-ENGLISH]. And then in the morning, he said, you can go out now. He didn't ask me much. He didn't want to get involved. Just, you can go out. Going out, I thought they wait for me somewhere. I felt that they around. They just wanted to find out exactly, and waited for me. So I didn't go straight away to the woman where my child was. And not far from there, there was a little round place with seats. Every Sunday, I know there are a few [INAUDIBLE] like this, every Sunday came in the girls and boys from country places, like peasants. And they came in there. They danced there. That was a little-- just a place with a few chairs. But it was empty then. And I sat on a chair-- on benches, small benches. I sat down on a bench. I had nothing else to do. I didn't want to go to the Polish woman. Also, I wanted to see my child. But I was afraid that they would go after me. So I sat there nearly a whole day. And it was evening. It came. It was dark. As I said, [INAUDIBLE] I was right. I felt it. And I was right. These two men, two policemen that went after me, suddenly they appeared there and sat down on the bench near me, one in one side, one the other side. And I knew I was right. If I would have gone there, they would be after me. Straight away, they would find out everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: I sat there, and said to me, like said, why don't you go home? I said, I can't. Why? They were interested. Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I haven't got money. I have to go by train. I'm from [NON-ENGLISH]. And it's quite distant from there. They wouldn't find out. They couldn't find out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said to them, could you give me-- help me out? Could you give me some money only for the train? Only to go back home, I haven't got any money with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came here to sell something, some bit of milk or butter. And they took me to prison. I didn't want to say that I recognize you. I know you after me from prison.

speaker: interviewee
text: Aren't you Jewish? I said, why should I be Jewish? You be-- you can be Jewish too. Aren't you Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, don't be clever, he said to me. You are Jewish. I said, you want me to be Jewish. But I'm not. Otherwise, they wouldn't let me out of prison. And you know that I've been in prison. You know. And you have seen me. They released me from prison because I'm not. They wouldn't release a Jewish woman from prison. So he could-- they could see they can't go far with me. So another one got near me, started near me. I said, don't do that, because I knew it was a talk before that no rape [INAUDIBLE] it was not allowed in Germany, not with Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, you know that's not allowed to do it. You said I'm Jewish. And I say that you are Jewish. Why you are doing-- why are you coming just very near me?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, otherwise, all right, I'll go with you. And I knew that they were married men. Take me to your place. Then you can do anything you want to me. Just take me to your place. I want to go with you. I rather like you. They couldn't do anything. And the other one said, leave her alone. Just leave her. They left me for good after this. They tried everything. They couldn't get far. So they left me. As they left me, I took a deep breath. And I thought, no, in middle of the night, I'm not going. They left me for good. They couldn't do anything. Or they believed me. I acted, you know, as a non-- not a Jewish woman. But I couldn't go home to see my daughter. So I waited all night. In the morning, I had a good look around. They were nowhere to see them. And I started to walk. If they would see me walking two hours, they would be behind me. No one there, they left me. They had enough.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went in. And as I went in, the child started to cry, and said, Mommy, Mommy, [? Trisha, ?] the name of her daughter, of the woman's daughter. she was 9 years old, was [? Trisha. ?] [? Trisha ?] said that the German killed mommy. Mommy's dead.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as she told me this, that when happened that the Polish woman, [NON-ENGLISH] was her name, said, look, now as they went after you-- and all night I told her this story. Now, you have to go out. And that was in the morning. When I went out on the street, my child was crying. And I couldn't go even to kiss her. She said out, out, go out, because they will kill us all if you stay here longer. Just go out. And then they will send you-- from the streets, they surround people walking on the street. Anyone walking on the streets, they took them, if they didn't work at home. And then when it happened, I had-- they surrounded us, and took us a group of people among non-Jews. I don't know. Maybe were some Jews. I have no idea. They took us to another place where we had to stay for about two or three days, I remember, until they had enough people for a transport. We had to be about 800,000 people to send to Germany. From time to time, they had to get such a number of people and send to all different parts of Germany. And that's why I said before that we had to go through an officer, and that Ukrainian helped me, the person behind the desk who gave us all documents to go to Germany to be sent to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. We'll continue the story next time.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I rem--
